description: On a path, a person or another animal can 'thalo'. Alternating steps carry the body forward while the feet keep returning to the ground. The word names the walking gait rather than motion in general: 'roke' (move) can shift anything, and 'rashelo' (run) has a different pattern of support.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip at or just behind the upper teeth and let unvoiced air pass for dental 'th', then open into stressed central 'a'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l', let the voice pass around its sides, and round the lips for middle-height back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: A soft dental breath opens the word, then the lateral of the second syllable lets the voice pass around the tongue. The two even beats can recall one foot following the other.
usage_notes: The walker is the subject. A setting takes 'mua' (LOC), while surface contact takes 'nia' (ON). An open direction takes 'wea' (TOWARD), and a reached destination takes 'kau' (ALL).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Walking meditation makes this ordinary gait a direct practice term. Phi's four-posture teaching places it beside 'whano' (stand), 'meilo' (sit), and 'ruemi' (lie down).

semantic_domains.activity: ordinary travel and practice on foot
semantic_domains.physical: alternating bodily support on the feet
semantic_domains.spatial: a walking route across a place
